quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to atomic radius as you move from left to right across a period?

options.text: It increases because more electrons are added to the atom
It decreases because more protons pull electrons closer in
It stays the same because the period does not affect size
It alternates between larger and smaller across the period

quiz_questions.explanation: Atomic radius decreases across a period because the number of protons increases, pulling the electrons closer to the nucleus. The electrons are added to the same energy level, so they do not shield each other effectively.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to atomic radius as you move down a group?

options.text: It decreases because the nucleus gets stronger
It stays the same throughout the entire group
It increases because more electron shells are added
It first increases then decreases going down the group

quiz_questions.explanation: Atomic radius increases going down a group because each element has an additional electron energy level (shell), making the atom physically larger.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which element has the largest atomic radius: lithium, sodium, or potassium?

options.text: Lithium, because it is at the top of the group
Sodium, because it is in the middle of the group
Potassium, because it is lowest in the group
They all have the same size atomic radius value

quiz_questions.explanation: Potassium has the largest atomic radius because it is the lowest of the three in Group 1, with the most electron shells (4 shells vs. 3 for sodium and 2 for lithium).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which element has a smaller atomic radius: sodium or chlorine?

options.text: Sodium, because it has fewer protons than chlorine
Chlorine, because it has more protons pulling electrons in
They are the same size since they are in the same period
Sodium, because it is a metal and metals are always smaller

quiz_questions.explanation: Chlorine has a smaller atomic radius than sodium even though they are in the same period. Chlorine has 17 protons pulling on its electrons versus sodium's 11, pulling electrons closer.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which element on the periodic table has the smallest atomic radius?

options.text: Francium
Hydrogen
Helium
Lithium

quiz_questions.explanation: Helium has the smallest atomic radius. It is in the top right corner of the periodic table with only 2 protons pulling on its 2 electrons in a single shell.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which element has the largest atomic radius on the periodic table?

options.text: Fluorine, at the top right of the table
Helium, at the top right corner of the table
Cesium, at the bottom left area of the table
Neon, in the top right section of the table

quiz_questions.explanation: Cesium (Cs) has the largest atomic radius of the naturally occurring elements. It is near the bottom left of the periodic table with 6 electron shells and relatively few protons pulling on the outer electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What unit is commonly used to measure atomic radius?

options.text: Picometers (pm) or angstroms
Centimeters (cm) or meters (m)
Grams (g) or kilograms (kg)
Liters (L) or milliliters (mL)

quiz_questions.explanation: Atomic radius is measured in picometers (pm) or angstroms because atoms are extremely small. One picometer is one trillionth of a meter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does the atomic radius decrease across a period even though electrons are being added?

options.text: Electrons are removed as you move across a period on the table
Protons increase and pull all electrons closer to the nucleus
Neutrons increase and push electrons further from the nucleus
The electron shells decrease in number as you move across the table

quiz_questions.explanation: Although electrons are added across a period, they go into the same energy level. Meanwhile, more protons are added to the nucleus, increasing its positive charge and pulling all electrons closer.

quiz_questions.question_text: Comparing elements in the same period, which type of element generally has the largest atomic radius?

options.text: Noble gases on the far right side
Metals on the far left side of the table
Halogens in Group 17 of the table
Metalloids along the staircase line area

quiz_questions.explanation: Within the same period, metals on the left side have the largest atomic radius because they have fewer protons pulling on their electrons, so the electrons are less tightly held.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the number of electron shells relate to atomic radius?

options.text: More shells means a smaller radius always
The number of shells has no effect at all
More shells means a larger radius generally
Fewer shells means a larger radius generally

quiz_questions.explanation: More electron shells generally means a larger atomic radius because each additional shell extends the electron cloud farther from the nucleus, making the atom bigger.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is atomic radius?

options.text: Half the distance between the nuclei of two bonded identical atoms
The total number of electrons orbiting around the nucleus of an atom
The charge of the nucleus divided by the number of electron shells
The weight of a single atom measured in atomic mass units on a scale

quiz_questions.explanation: Atomic radius is defined as half the distance between the nuclei of two identical atoms that are bonded together. It represents the size of an atom.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'periodic trend' mean?

options.text: A predictable pattern in element properties across the periodic table
A random change in properties that occurs without any pattern at all
A chemical reaction that happens periodically at regular time intervals
The order in which new elements are added to the periodic table today

quiz_questions.explanation: A periodic trend is a predictable, regular pattern in the properties of elements as you move across a period or down a group on the periodic table.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'nuclear charge' mean?

options.text: The total positive charge from all protons in the nucleus of an atom
The negative charge created by all electrons orbiting around the nucleus
The energy released when the nucleus of an atom splits apart in fission
The distance between the nucleus and the outermost electron in an atom

quiz_questions.explanation: Nuclear charge is the total positive charge of the nucleus, determined by the number of protons. A higher nuclear charge means a stronger pull on electrons, which affects atomic radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron shielding' mean?

options.text: Inner electrons block the full nuclear pull from reaching outer electrons
Outer electrons protect inner electrons from chemical reactions with others
Electrons form a physical barrier around the nucleus preventing it from splitting
The nucleus pushes electrons away from itself to prevent them from collapsing

quiz_questions.explanation: Electron shielding (or screening) occurs when inner electron shells partially block the attractive force of the nucleus from reaching the outer electrons. More inner shells means more shielding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is effective nuclear charge?

options.text: The net positive charge felt by outer electrons after shielding is considered
The total number of protons and neutrons inside the nucleus of any atom
The energy needed to completely remove the nucleus from inside an atom
The charge that remains on an atom after all electrons have been removed

quiz_questions.explanation: Effective nuclear charge is the net positive charge experienced by valence electrons after accounting for the shielding effect of inner electrons. It influences atomic radius and other properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'atomic size' mean?

options.text: The overall dimensions of an atom based on its electron cloud boundaries
The mass of all protons and neutrons inside the nucleus of the atom only
The number of energy levels or shells that an atom contains in its structure
The number of bonds an atom can form with other atoms in a compound made

quiz_questions.explanation: Atomic size refers to the overall dimensions of an atom, determined by the extent of its electron cloud. It is directly related to and often used interchangeably with atomic radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electron cloud' mean?

options.text: The region around the nucleus where electrons are most likely to be found
A cloud-shaped formation that appears when atoms react in a gas phase
The physical boundary that separates one atom from all neighboring atoms
A visible cloud that forms around hot metals when they start to cool down

quiz_questions.explanation: The electron cloud is the three-dimensional region around the nucleus where electrons are most likely to be found. It defines the general size and shape of an atom.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'energy level' mean in atomic structure?

options.text: A specific region around the nucleus where electrons of similar energy reside
The amount of heat energy needed to melt a solid element into a liquid state
The total kinetic energy of all protons moving inside the nucleus of the atom
The brightness of light emitted when an element is burned in a flame test run

quiz_questions.explanation: An energy level (or electron shell) is a region around the nucleus where electrons with a specific amount of energy are found. Higher energy levels are farther from the nucleus.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a covalent radius?

options.text: Half the distance between the nuclei of two atoms joined by a covalent bond
The distance between the nucleus and the outermost electron of a free atom
The total number of covalent bonds that a single atom is able to form overall
The charge an atom gains or loses when it forms an ionic bond with another atom

quiz_questions.explanation: A covalent radius is half the distance between the nuclei of two identical atoms that are bonded by a covalent bond. It is one way to measure atomic radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'trend across a period' mean?

options.text: A pattern observed in element properties moving left to right on a period row
A change in properties that happens when moving from top to bottom in a group
A random shift in element behavior that has no predictable direction at all
The total number of elements contained within a single period on the table

quiz_questions.explanation: A trend across a period describes the predictable pattern of how element properties change as you move from left to right along a horizontal row of the periodic table.

quiz_questions.question_text: Element A is in Period 2, Group 1, and Element B is in Period 2, Group 17. Which element has the larger atomic radius and why?

options.text: Element B, because it has more electrons in its outer electron shell
Element A, because it has fewer protons pulling its electrons inward
They are the same size because they are both in the same period row
Element B, because nonmetals are always larger than metals in a period

quiz_questions.explanation: Element A (lithium) has a larger atomic radius than Element B (fluorine). Both are in Period 2, but lithium has only 3 protons while fluorine has 9, pulling its electrons much closer.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is comparing the atomic radius of oxygen and sulfur. Both are in Group 16, but sulfur is in Period 3 while oxygen is in Period 2. Which is larger?

options.text: Oxygen is larger because it has fewer protons in its nucleus overall
They are the same size because they are in the same group on the table
Sulfur is larger because it has more electron shells than oxygen does
Oxygen is larger because it is higher on the periodic table than sulfur

quiz_questions.explanation: Sulfur has a larger atomic radius than oxygen because it has 3 electron shells compared to oxygen's 2. Going down a group, atomic radius increases due to additional electron shells.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student says that francium must be a very large atom because it is at the bottom left of the periodic table. Is this reasoning correct?

options.text: No, atom size has nothing to do with position on the periodic table at all
No, atoms at the bottom left are actually the smallest atoms on the table
Yes, atoms at the bottom left have the most shells and fewest pulling protons
Yes, but only because francium is the heaviest element on the periodic table

quiz_questions.explanation: The student is correct. Atomic radius increases going down (more shells) and going left (fewer protons pulling). The bottom-left corner has the largest atoms, and francium is among the largest.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sodium (Na) has an atomic radius of 186 pm, and its ion Na+ has a radius of 95 pm. Why does the sodium ion have a much smaller radius?

options.text: The ion has one fewer electron shell and more protons pulling the remaining electrons
The ion gains extra protons that squeeze the atom into a smaller physical space
The ion has absorbed all of its electrons into the nucleus making it much smaller
The measurement must be incorrect because ions are always the same size as atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: When sodium loses its 1 valence electron to form Na+, it loses its entire outer shell. The remaining electrons are pulled closer by the 11 protons, so the ion is much smaller than the neutral atom.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chlorine (Cl) has an atomic radius of 99 pm, but its ion Cl- has a radius of 181 pm. Why does the chloride ion have a larger radius?

options.text: The extra electron increases repulsion between electrons expanding the cloud
The chloride ion gained extra protons that push the electrons further outward
Negative ions are always exactly double the size of the neutral atom always
The chloride ion has an additional electron shell that the neutral atom lacks

quiz_questions.explanation: When chlorine gains an electron to form Cl-, the extra electron increases electron-electron repulsion while the nuclear charge stays the same. This causes the electron cloud to expand, increasing the radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist needs to choose between aluminum and gallium for a project. Both are in Group 13, but gallium is one period below aluminum. Which atom is larger?

options.text: Aluminum, because it has fewer electrons causing its shells to spread out more
They are exactly the same size because they are in the same group on the table
Gallium, because it has one more electron shell than aluminum does in its atom
Aluminum, because metals higher in a group are always larger than those below

quiz_questions.explanation: Gallium has a larger atomic radius because it is below aluminum in Group 13 with an additional electron shell. Atomic radius increases going down a group.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you were to line up all the elements in Period 3 by atomic radius from largest to smallest, which element would come first?

options.text: Chlorine, because it has the most electrons in Period 3
Silicon, because it is in the middle of the period row
Argon, because noble gases are the largest elements always
Sodium, because it is farthest left with the fewest protons

quiz_questions.explanation: Sodium has the largest atomic radius in Period 3 because it is farthest left with only 11 protons. As you move right, more protons pull electrons closer, decreasing the radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: A textbook states that bromine has a larger atomic radius than chlorine. Both are halogens. Using periodic trends, explain why.

options.text: Bromine has more valence electrons than chlorine, making it wider overall
Bromine is below chlorine with an additional electron shell making it bigger
Bromine is to the right of chlorine on the table so it must be much bigger
Bromine has fewer protons so its nucleus pulls electrons less tightly inward

quiz_questions.explanation: Bromine (Period 4) is below chlorine (Period 3) in Group 17. It has one additional electron shell, making its atomic radius larger despite having more protons.

quiz_questions.question_text: Magnesium and calcium are both alkaline earth metals. A student predicts calcium is larger because it is lower in the group. Is this prediction consistent with periodic trends?

options.text: No, elements lower in a group are actually smaller due to more protons present
No, atomic radius does not change going down a group on the periodic table
Yes, calcium has more electron shells making it larger than magnesium overall
Yes, but only because calcium has fewer electrons than magnesium in its atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: The student's prediction is correct. Calcium is in Period 4 while magnesium is in Period 3. The additional electron shell in calcium makes it larger, consistent with the trend of increasing atomic radius down a group.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student claims that because carbon has more protons than boron, carbon must be smaller. However, both are in Period 2. Is the student correct?

options.text: Yes, carbon has more protons pulling its electrons closer making it smaller
No, carbon is larger because having more protons always increases atomic size
No, elements in the same period always have the exact same atomic radius
Yes, but only because carbon has fewer electron shells than boron does today

quiz_questions.explanation: The student is correct. Carbon (6 protons) has more nuclear charge than boron (5 protons), pulling its electrons closer. Both are in Period 2 with the same number of shells, so the extra protons make carbon smaller.
